items.title: Prove correctness or pick a counter-example
items.prefer: Either prove the greedy-choice + optimal-substructure properties, or stress-test against brute force / DP on small inputs.
items.why: Wrong greedies pass simple tests and ship.
items.example:
```python
# Stress test:
import random
for _ in range(10_000):
    case = generate()
    assert greedy(case) == optimal_brute(case)
```

items.title: Lead with a sort by the right key
items.prefer: Identify what makes a choice 'local best': deadline, ratio, score; sort by that.
items.why: The sort key IS the greedy strategy.
items.example:
```python
# Activity selection: sort by END time, not start.
intervals.sort(key=lambda x: x[1])
```

items.title: Use a priority queue when 'best so far' changes
items.avoid: Re-sorting the whole collection on every step.
items.prefer: `heapq` for log-n updates of the locally best choice.
items.why: Sorts the whole list each step is O(n² log n); a priority queue gives O((V+E) log V).

items.title: Distinguish 'provably optimal greedy' from 'good-enough heuristic'
items.avoid: Calling a heuristic 'optimal' just because it's greedy.
items.prefer: State explicitly when an algorithm is heuristic (greedy decoding, prompt-budget allocation) and measure quality.
items.why: Heuristics need eval; optimal algorithms come with a proof.
items.example:
```python
# Heuristic example: allocate token budget to highest-impact section first.
```

items.title: Fall back to DP when greedy fails
items.avoid: Patching greedy with ad-hoc rules for special cases.
items.prefer: Switch to dynamic programming once you've shown greedy is wrong.
items.why: DP is the principled answer; ad-hoc patches usually re-introduce bugs.
